title: How To Reduce The Risk Of Occluding The Columella Artery
filename: How To Reduce The Risk Of Occluding The Columella Artery.en.vtt
excerpt: Hello everyone and it's been a while since I've done a Facebook live and I wanted to do one today on a significant event occurred over the weekend so this is someone who I don't even know through an internet forum had a problem with an occlusion and dealt with it extremely well a…

segments.text: hello everyone and it's been a while since I've done a Facebook live and I wanted to do one today on a significant event occurred over the weekend so this is someone who I don't even know through an internet forum had a problem with an occlusion and dealt with it extremely well and first occlusion as it usually is because most of us don't get very many of these and need a lot of support in terms of dealing with it had a really rough weekend you know when these things happen you really got to step up and look after your patient and go the extra mile so multiple visits and treatments and you know an entire weekend devoted to solving this problem which i think is amazing and that's exactly what all good clinicians should do as you stop everything else when you solve the problem so and massive kudos for that finition but the last email that I had from her last message was as we often do we often feel really bad about these situations and the question she asked was how can I avoid this happening I feel bad what

should I have done differently and actually I think that question is a crucial question that every professional should answer so you should ask yourself whenever something bad happens we all need to go and ask ourselves what could we have done better and what I thought might be helpful would be for me to share some of my ideas how about how your injection technique could be made safer particularly when injecting the philtrum so this is where I think this injection that caused the problem came from hi Julian and hi Jenny and because the little area the collusion that occurred was about here so it's just about here at the base of the nose and a little bit over here so the injection that caused it could either have been a filter or of a million border injection along here or it could have been a footer column injection here so both of those injections are aesthetically required I think so I do occasionally hear of training schools that don't teach

doing the filter columns but first I think if you don't treat a filter column on everyone eventually you're gonna use some of your patients will end up looking a bit duck mailed is that projection of the pink part of the lip with nothing connecting it properly to the nose looks a bit strange a little bit false so I do think it's a necessary injection so how can we do it while keeping the total risk as low as possible and first thing to think about is to look at the anatomy that's involved in that area you basically have I built this little model last night and just to show you so we've obviously got the labial artery and the superior labral artery branching off the facial or the facial artery it's usually very close to the Vermilion border but can dip into the pink part of the lip and it should be and usually is underneath the orbicularis Oris muscle so that helps you that tells you depth is very important for most people now unfortunately sometimes it comes a little bit out of that muscle and it

isn't 100% of the time always where we would like it to be which is away from my needle so it can be a little bit close to the surface but it shouldn't really be in the surface you know it's never something that's the first thing after the dermis do otherwise you'd built feel it pulsating is not actually a very small artery it's actually a reasonable size doctor we should be able to feel it if it was too superficial but that's useful to know and then and throws off a branch which is the columellar artery which runs up in the surprise the tip of the nose and this is also why you should always if anyone has a lip treatment and you're worried about an occlusion ask them about painful pain in their nose and if you're ever checking to make sure you haven't caused delusion is always to check the tip of the nose and the philtrum when you're looking for capillary refill which is one of the learning points from this case so and if you're going to enhance the filter columns hopefully mostly the columellar artery is in the midline but it isn't always and unfortunately it also crosses the philtrum and there are also ascending filter arteries which are much smaller but more superficial so you can actually include little vessels in the philtrum as well but they're

much smaller than the columellar artery but that they can be significant to know about so if you're going to inject this area how are we going to do it to make it safer I think one of the things that makes it a bit tricky is the fact that we have this curvature to it so that there is a curve here on most people not on every one where you can actually see that the artery is not straight it's slightly u-shaped which is actually pretty if you have a u-shaped philtrum and then congratulate yourself because it's a nice shape when they get as you get older they tend to get straight as you can see in this model though philtrum is a little bit straighter but in young and basically the ideal folks from what would be slightly curved but that curvature has with it a little bit of potential risk because the artery is no longer in the exact shape of an eagle so if you're going to stick a needle in the philtrum even if your superficial hopefully you can see that there's a chance if you go a little bit deeper that you're going to include that artery because going at the right depth but in a u-shape you're going to get deeper as you get further

in so that would be one of the things I think you can do to make it safer is don't go unnecessarily deep if you have a look at this model most people have a relative fee and at this point a little bit fuller so you don't necessarily need to augment that I think you can make this the key aesthetic difference by treating the lower maybe the lower two thirds of it rather than going all the way in and I know that when we're training and we tend to get positive aspirations here so if you if you're sliding a needle in all the way here so let's actually put it in so a deep injection like that and you aspirate that's usually where you get your positive now as you come out obviously you're going to be coming out of the vessel and you may not get positive but you may which is the next thing on to talk about which is what can you do to improve your aspirating this is a risky area it's risky for one really big reason which is your needle is running parallel with the artery just like it isn't a familiar border together so any at any point where your needle is parallel with the artery you've got a greater chance of

cannulating it and then you've got a greater chance of causing an occlusion so what we should do is do something to increase the sensitivity of your aspiration there because when you aspirate you're only checking one point so you know that at that point doesn't it you're not fit you know there's less chance of you being in our tree precise operations never a hundred percent sensitive but it's sensitive enough that you can be a little bit reassured if you get a negative but as soon as you if you're doing a linear thread and sliding out you're going to be out to that blood vessel again so what if you didn't pay all that what if it is a situation like this where you've gone in and gone all the way through the artery you aspirate that's negative and then you start to inject well at some point you're going to include that artery even though you've aspirated so what I started doing in risky areas is aspirating multiple times when you aspirate all the time it's incredibly it's it takes no time at all I don't really mind it doesn't slow me down it's not significant a lot of people when you first start it feels really it's annoying and you and if you're not

used to aspirating it's fiddly and it's slow and you move the needle around and it causes pain for your clients and then you often stop aspirating for that reason and you come up with some justification why you don't need to but actually I think if you force yourself to aspirate a lot you get very quick at it so I did a little aspiration with my left hand as I'm injecting and you can do multiple and let's see if I can actually do one so I can show you so if I was in philtrum I'd be doing something like this so nice and superficial you want to be very superficial because it's more superficial you are the less chance of you being in an artery but you'd only be in the epidermis or you will you can either cause a pressure symptom or you can get terminal effects you see the fellows who want to be nice and superficial and lift the lift to do death check and look for blanching on the surface of the skin but when you're a neutral you shouldn't see any blanching and then especially if you will try not to go too deep so if you go

kind of 2/3 of the way in that should be deep enough to get your aesthetic result and then a scrape then you can do a little injection come out and I spray again little injection a spray and that's obviously going to create a little tiny bonuses so I give it a little squeeze afterwards to shape it the way you want it to be and that should be a little bit safer so in summary multiple aspirations don't go all the way deep into where the column at a common man that artery will be and start about 2/3 of it we go about 2/3 of the way in as in most cases and unless you've got a very flat angle here and do multiple aspirations soup as you come out and do little tiny bonuses and then shape it with your finger I think that's probably one of the safer way of doing it so with nothings a hundred percent safe but we just dialed it up a little bit made it a little bit safer you can still treat the philtrum you're still going to get a better aesthetic result but not treating the philtrum and hopefully our chances of including it

are decreased and I do recommend after every treatment that you just do a little check just check for you know pinch the nose to check the tip of the nose if you're concerned do with a cotton bud look familiar refilled in the philtrum and check the lips themselves they're usually the easiest thing to check because they're bright red and if you include them you get a little white patch so yeah I hope that helps you get even safer and if you have any other ideas about how to make it safer or questions on how to do it please comment below and aspirating always causes a little debate a lot of people think it's there's no point doing it because it's not a hundred percent effective I always say that's a bit like a seat belt it's also not a hundred percent in fact but it's certainly better and nothing hello-hello Williams and so tag anyone who you think might find this useful and share it if you think it's useful and I really appreciate your comments so anything you write down below I will read it appreciate it and

try and get back to you as soon as possible if there are questions or I'll do them in a future video but thank you very much for watching I hope that was helpful see you on the next Facebook blog which will probably be Friday but I might try and do one tomorrow thanks for watching everyone
